evidence: Clear layered dependency: model/utils (no deps) → config (→ utils) → query (→ model, utils) → exploration (→ query, model, config) → api (→ all). No upward references found.

ExplorationHandlers.java imports 9 individual model types (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions, ExplorationResult) and has parallel format* methods (formatSeedCollocate, formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatEdge) that duplicate serialization already available in ExplorationResult.toMap() and ComparisonResult.toMap().

WordSketchApiServer uses com.sun.net.httpserver via fully-qualified type references (com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer, com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler) inline rather than imports — the JDK-internal transport layer bleeds into the server wiring code.
query package: BlackLabSnippetParser and CollocateQueryHelper are package-private helpers that exist solely for BlackLabQueryExecutor. This is sound design — they are bounded within the package.
No import cycles found. Each package imports only from packages lower in the stack.

SemanticFieldExplorer (exploration package) directly receives and stores a QueryExecutor (query package). The exploration package thus depends on the query interface, which is correct — no back-dependency.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Serialization responsibility is split: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult have toMap() methods, yet ExplorationHandlers re-implements equivalent formatting via private format* methods. This creates two authoritative serialization paths for the same domain types, blurring the boundary between model and API presentation layers.

evidence: Package layout maps to domain capabilities: model (DTOs), config (grammar loading), query (BlackLab execution), exploration (semantic analysis), api (HTTP surface), indexer (corpus indexing), utils (shared primitives), viz (plot data). New engineers can orient quickly.
CLAUDE.md states 'SemanticFieldExplorer.java' is under 'query/' but it actually lives in 'exploration/'. Documentation has drifted from runtime reality.

QueryResults outer class holds four unrelated inner types (WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult). Other model types are all top-level records. QueryResults is a structural outlier that obscures ownership.

ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult both have toMap() serialization methods; ExplorationHandlers duplicates this with format* private methods — unclear whether model or api layer owns JSON serialization.

ExploreOptions record (named 'Explore') vs ExplorationResult class (named 'Exploration') — asymmetry documented as intentional in ExploreOptions Javadoc but still creates cognitive friction for newcomers.

AdjectiveCollocateRanker in exploration package: the name says 'ranker' but the class performs multi-seed comparison (compareCollocateProfiles, buildAdjectiveProfiles). The responsibility is wider than the name suggests.
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc lists all 14 endpoints inline — this is a good practice and matches runtime reality.
README.md endpoint table matches actual registered routes in WordSketchApiServer, confirming docs-runtime alignment for the HTTP surface.
All production classes use standard Java camelCase for methods and fields; PascalCase for types. No snake_case or kebab-case exceptions found in Java sources.

Static utility classes with private constructors are used consistently: CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils, BlackLabSnippetParser, PatternSubstitution. All follow the same shape.

Package-private visibility used consistently for handler classes: ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, RelationUtils, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper are all package-private (no public modifier). This is a deliberate, consistent convention.

QueryResults outer class is a structural outlier: all other model types (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, ExploreOptions, RelationEdgeType) are top-level classes/records, but result types (WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult) are nested inside QueryResults.

WordSketchApiServer uses fully-qualified names for com.sun.net.httpserver types (com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer, com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler, com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange) inline in method signatures rather than import statements. All other classes in the codebase use import statements for external types.
ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult document the record vs. class naming convention difference explicitly in Javadoc — both are consistent with the convention they describe.

Record types (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions, QueryResults inner records) consistently omit the 'get' prefix for accessors. Traditional classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) consistently use 'get' prefix. Convention is documented and followed.

LongIntHashMap in utils/ is a specialized data structure, not a utility function. The utils package otherwise contains pure function utilities (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) and a CQL helper (CqlUtils). LongIntHashMap is a minor outlier in the utils package.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: QueryResults nested-type pattern contradicts the established top-level record convention in the model package. WordSketchApiServer's inline fully-qualified type names for com.sun.net.httpserver break the consistent import style used by every other class.

evidence: Consistent wrapping of checked exceptions: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor wraps Exception as IOException with cause; executeQuery wraps InvalidQuery as IOException with cause; CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery and executeCollocateSearch wrap InvalidQuery as IOException. Cause chain is preserved.
BlackLabConllUIndexer.close() catches Exception and wraps as IOException — consistent with constructor pattern.

getTotalFrequency in CollocateQueryHelper catches RuntimeException and rethrows as IllegalStateException (unchecked) instead of IOException. This is an inconsistency: all other I/O methods declare throws IOException, but this one throws an unchecked exception that callers cannot anticipate.

buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution in BlackLabQueryExecutor throws IllegalArgumentException (unchecked) for an unrecognized CQL pattern format. The QueryExecutor interface documents that findCollocations may throw IllegalArgumentException, but this contract is not documented for executeSurfacePattern or other callers. The exception escapes callers that only catch IOException.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType catches Exception broadly per individual relation (catch (Exception e)) — this silently swallows unexpected bugs as relation-level errors and logs them as warnings, not errors. Individual relation failures are collected as strings and included in the response.
ExplorationHandlers handlers uniformly catch IOException (-> 500) and IllegalArgumentException (-> 400) separately. This is a consistent, deliberate strategy for the HTTP boundary.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern throws IllegalArgumentException for missing relationType or unsupported PosGroup. This is appropriate input validation, but the IllegalArgumentException can escape the IOException catch in ExplorationHandlers if thrown after the try block — however, inspection shows it is thrown before the try block, so it is caught correctly.

BlackLabSnippetParser methods return null for absent values (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition). Call sites in BlackLabQueryExecutor consistently null-check these returns. Null-return strategy is used only within the query package; no nulls leak across package boundaries.

CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates returns an empty list when no collocates are found — uses empty list (not null, not Optional) as the 'no results' signal. This is consistent across all methods in the query layer.

LogDiceCalculator.compute returns UNCOMPUTABLE (Double.NaN) for invalid inputs. Callers in CollocateQueryHelper use Double.isNaN to gate on this sentinel. The sentinel is documented. However, callers in the exploration layer (SemanticFieldExplorer) call executor methods that already filtered NaN; the sentinel does not leak further up.

Method names are largely domain-aligned and action-revealing: findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, buildAdjectiveProfiles — all communicate intent clearly.

Variable 'cs' used for CollocateQueryHelper.CollocateSearch in three places in BlackLabQueryExecutor: findCollocations line 65, runDependencyQuery line 176, executeSurfacePattern line 214. 'cs' is opaque; the type name CollocateSearch is not well-known enough to justify the abbreviation.

handleSemanticFieldExplore (single-seed endpoint) uses requireParam(exchange, params, 'seeds') and assigns to variable 'seed' (singular). The query parameter is named 'seeds' (plural) but accepts only one value. This name/behavior mismatch can confuse API clients expecting multiple seeds.

NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT constant in SemanticFieldExplorer is named for its structural role (CQL constraint) but not its field type (xpos-based). The actual value is '[xpos="NN.*"]'. A name like NOUN_XPOS_CONSTRAINT or NOUN_SURFACE_PATTERN would distinguish it from a deprel-based constraint.

nounsParam local variable in handleSemanticFieldComparison (ExplorationHandlers line 148) stores the value of the 'seeds' query parameter but is named 'nounsParam'. The parameter renamed from 'nouns' to 'seeds' in the API but the internal variable was not updated — name/intent mismatch.

Private method names in ExplorationHandlers are consistent and well-chosen: resolveRelationConfig, resolveExploreParams, buildBaseExploreResponse, buildExploreResponseBody, parseSeedSet — all accurately describe what they do.
Deprecated getEdges() method was replaced by buildEdges() — the new name correctly signals that a list is constructed on every call, which is a good naming evolution.
Pattern variable naming in RelationConfig: 'headPosition' (field), 'collocatePosition()' (method), 'headPosConstraint' (parameter) — these are consistent and clear.
CollocateQueryHelper inner record 'CollocateSearch' returned by executeCollocateSearch accurately names the result type. Its fields headwordFreq() and groups() are clear.
Private field 'collocateQueryHelper' in BlackLabQueryExecutor accurately names the collaborator. No generic names like 'helper' or 'manager' found at the field level.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Variable 'cs' is an opaque abbreviation for CollocateSearch used three times in BlackLabQueryExecutor. The 'seeds' vs 'seed' mismatch in the single-seed endpoint creates a name/contract confusion. 'nounsParam' in handleSemanticFieldComparison is a stale name from a parameter rename.

QueryExecutor interface defines: findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults), executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults), executeDependencyPattern(lemma, deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) — consistent 4-arg signature with minLogDice.

executeBcqlQuery(bcqlPattern, maxResults) breaks the pattern: no lemma, no minLogDice. Callers who want logDice filtering must post-filter results manually, while all other query methods filter at query time.

executeQuery(cqlPattern, maxResults) also has no minLogDice param and returns ConcordanceResult (not WordSketchResult), which is a different result type than all other methods — the interface mixes two result shapes.

The deprecated 3-arg executeDependencyPattern(lemma, deprel, headPosConstraint, minLogDice, maxResults) is still part of the public interface. The @Deprecated annotation and Javadoc are clear but the method remains in the interface and must be implemented by test stubs.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore requires a query parameter named 'seeds' (plural) even though it accepts only a single seed and stores it in a variable named 'seed'. The URL schema is: ?seeds=house (singular value, plural name).

REST URL inconsistency: sketch endpoints use path parameters (/api/sketch/{lemma}) while exploration endpoints use query parameters (?seed=house). The mixing of conventions is documented but not enforced by any abstraction.

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams uses String.split('=', 2) — correct — but does not URL-decode keys, only values. A percent-encoded parameter name would silently fail to match. (Minor but an API boundary issue.)
WordSketchApiServer binds only to loopback without documentation at the API boundary — callers attempting to expose the API on a network interface will get a connection-refused without a clear error.
The application is a single-user, localhost-only research tool. There is no user authentication system, no session management, and no per-user data — all endpoints are intentionally public.
WordSketchApiServer binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — OS-level network isolation substitutes for application-level auth. This is an architectural auth decision.
Input validation is applied consistently in SketchHandlers: requireParam for lemma, word1, word2; numeric parsing with 400 on NumberFormatException for limit.

ExplorationHandlers validates: seeds not empty (400), seeds >= 2 for multi-seed (400), numeric params with NumberFormatException catch (400), relation config existence (400), relationType presence (400).
No endpoint validates an upper bound on numeric parameters: limit, top, maxResults can be arbitrarily large (e.g., ?top=1000000), potentially causing memory pressure or extremely long queries.
CORS is hardcoded to http://localhost:3000. Any request from a different origin is rejected by the browser. This is a consistent policy but hardcoded.
All response paths set the same CORS header via HttpApiUtils (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — consistent application of the CORS policy.

handleBcqlQueryPost reads a raw JSON body and passes the pattern directly to executeBcqlQuery — the only sanitization is what CqlUtils.escapeForRegex provides. However, BCQL patterns are parsed by BlackLab's own parser which will reject malformed input with InvalidQuery, which is caught and re-thrown as IOException.
No endpoints are documented as explicitly public health checks (the /health endpoint returns {status:ok} without validation — this is appropriate for a health check).
No RLS, no permission strings, no role system — domain is not applicable.

strengths: Strict one-directional dependency hierarchy: model → utils → config → query → exploration → api. No upward or circular imports.
QueryExecutor interface cleanly isolates the query subsystem; callers in exploration and api depend only on the interface, not on BlackLabQueryExecutor directly.
Package-private scope used correctly for BlackLabSnippetParser and CollocateQueryHelper — they are implementation details of the query package with no visibility to outer layers.
indexer.blacklab is self-contained and only called from Main — it does not leak into the serving path.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Serialization responsibility is split across the boundary between the model layer (toMap() methods on ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult) and the api layer (parallel format* private methods in ExplorationHandlers), creating two authoritative representations of the same data.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The package topology is clean and would score near 90 on dependency direction alone — no cycles, a clear stack, and good use of package-private encapsulation. The score is pulled down to 76 by the serialization duplication across the model/api boundary (a genuine boundary integrity issue, not just a style matter) and the use of a JDK-internal HTTP server (com.sun.net.httpserver) that couples the server architecture to JDK internals without abstraction.

strengths: Package structure maps clearly to domain capabilities (api, config, query, exploration, model, indexer, utils, viz) — a new engineer can predict where to look for any feature.
QueryExecutor interface provides a clean public surface for the query subsystem; implementations are hidden.
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc lists all endpoints, and they match the registered routes — the HTTP contract is honest.
model package uses Java records for immutable DTOs, consistent with modern Java idioms and clearly communicated in ComparisonResult's Javadoc.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary issue is ownership ambiguity: serialization responsibility is split between model toMap() methods and API handler format* methods, and CLAUDE.md has structural documentation that doesn't match actual package locations.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase earns a mid-70s score: the package decomposition is sound and would rank in the upper quartile for a Java project of this size, but the serialization ownership ambiguity blurs the model/api boundary, AdjectiveCollocateRanker undersells its own responsibility, and CLAUDE.md has a factual error about SemanticFieldExplorer's package location. These are real navigability costs for a new contributor, not style nits.

strengths: Java camelCase/PascalCase naming is uniform across all 32 source files with no exceptions.
Package-private handler classes (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, RelationUtils) form a consistent pattern across the api package.
Record vs. class accessor convention (no-get for records, get-prefix for classes) is documented and applied consistently.
Static utility class pattern (private constructor, all-static methods) is used uniformly for CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils, BlackLabSnippetParser, PatternSubstitution.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Two isolated outliers exist: the QueryResults wrapper class contradicts the top-level record pattern used everywhere else in the model package, and WordSketchApiServer uses inline fully-qualified type names where all other files use imports.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase scores 80 because the conventions are strong and well-documented — the naming, visibility, and accessor patterns are applied with discipline. The two outliers (QueryResults nested types, inline FQN in WordSketchApiServer) are real inconsistencies but they are isolated and do not represent systemic drift. A perfect score is not warranted because the QueryResults pattern actively misleads new contributors about the model package's structural convention.

strengths: IOException wrapping with cause preservation is applied consistently at all I/O boundaries (BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabConllUIndexer).
HTTP boundary error strategy is uniform across ExplorationHandlers: IOException maps to 500, IllegalArgumentException maps to 400.
Null returns are confined within the query package; no null results cross the package boundary into callers.
Empty list as the no-results sentinel is used consistently in the query layer.
error_consistency.issue_character: The primary issue is unchecked exception leakage: IllegalArgumentException and IllegalStateException are thrown in paths that callers expect to only throw IOException, creating unexpected failure modes that bypass caller error handling.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error strategy is well-designed at the HTTP boundary and the IOException wrapping is disciplined. The score lands at 68 because two concrete leakage paths exist: getTotalFrequency throws an unchecked IllegalStateException in a code path callers treat as IOException-only, and buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throws IllegalArgumentException which is not documented in the QueryExecutor interface contract for the methods that call it (executeSurfacePattern, findCollocations). These are real predictability failures, not style choices.

strengths: Domain-specific vocabulary is used consistently: lemma, collocate, logDice, deprel, bcql, xpos — all match the linguistics/corpus-query domain and are applied uniformly.

Method names accurately describe the action: executeSurfacePattern vs executeDependencyPattern vs findCollocations vs executeBcqlQuery — each name distinguishes the method's approach, not just its general category.
The record vs class accessor naming convention (no-get for records, get-prefix for classes) is applied consistently and documented.
Deprecated getEdges() → buildEdges() rename demonstrates good naming discipline: the new name reveals the cost (list construction on every call).
naming_quality.issue_character: A small number of isolated naming issues exist: one opaque abbreviation ('cs') used in three places, one stale variable name ('nounsParam'), and one API parameter name mismatch ('seeds' for a single-seed endpoint).
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase scores 79 on naming: the domain vocabulary is strong and method names are precise. The deductions come from three concrete issues that could mislead contributors: the 'cs' abbreviation is used in three separate methods (not a one-off), the 'seeds' parameter on a single-seed endpoint creates an API contract confusion, and 'nounsParam' is a ghost of a renamed parameter. None are severe but together they represent a consistent slight of naming discipline in specific files.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper genuinely earns its existence: it removes ~100 lines of duplicated search/scoring/ranking logic from four BlackLabQueryExecutor methods
HttpApiUtils enforces CORS and JSON-encoding policy uniformly across all routes — thin but honest
Record types for model objects (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge) use record syntax precisely where appropriate: immutable value objects with no added behaviour
Handler decomposition into SketchHandlers / ExplorationHandlers keeps the routing server lean and separates route dispatch from query orchestration
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The primary abstraction problem is structural duplication: two serialization paths for the same model (ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult) and three independent CQL lemma-substitution implementations — abstractions were added in one layer without removing or reusing the equivalent logic in another.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase has several abstractions that genuinely pay off (CollocateQueryHelper, HttpApiUtils, handler decomposition) but also two systemic duplication patterns that indicate abstractions were introduced without refactoring existing code. ExplorationResult.toMap() is dead serialization code relative to the handler path, and three independent CQL substitution implementations suggest the abstraction boundary was never properly decided. This sits comfortably in the 60–70 range: some strong leverage but recurring parallel-implementation debt.

strengths: Lean dependency footprint: only 4 runtime libraries, each serving a distinct purpose with no overlapping alternatives
No circular dependencies possible in a single-module Maven project
Test deps correctly scoped; no test libraries leaking into the production classpath
dependency_health.issue_character: Issues are operational rather than architectural: the BlackLab repository URL is a fragile external point-of-failure acknowledged in comments but not remediated, and version currency is mildly stale.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set itself is disciplined — no fat imports for light use, no competing libraries, no unused declarations. The score is pulled down by the GitHub raw-content repository URL, which is a real CI reliability risk (not hypothetical — the comment explicitly names failure modes), and by the use of com.sun.net.httpserver which relies on JDK internals rather than a proper HTTP server library. These are low-probability but high-impact concerns that keep the score below 85.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper three-phase pipeline (collectHits, scoreHits, rank) has clear intent and bounded scope per phase
BlackLabSnippetParser: consistent @Nullable discipline, single-responsibility static methods, uniform null-return convention
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens() handles nested brackets and quoted strings with clean depth-counting — the control flow is direct and intention-revealing
SemanticFieldExplorer private phase helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) make each exploration step independently navigable
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary low-level problem is scattered diagnostic noise and inconsistent editing discipline: duplicated Javadoc, debug-logging woven through algorithm steps, and arg-parsing style inconsistency across sibling methods — all symptoms of accumulated edits without a consistent cleanup pass.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase has genuinely well-crafted internals in the query and parsing layers (CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, CqlUtils), which represent clean, precise function bodies. However the exploration and main CLI layers show accumulated clutter: duplicated documentation, verbose debug narrative woven through business logic, and inconsistent style across structurally identical methods. This puts the score in the 60–65 range — clear pockets of quality alongside visible craft debt.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is a clean seam: exploration and handler packages depend only on the interface, never on BlackLab types directly
ExploreOptions record removes a 5-argument method boundary and makes the tuning contract explicit
buildBaseExploreResponse() + buildExploreResponseBody() correctly factor out common response structure shared by single-seed and multi-seed paths
resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() extract repeated handler preambles into named boundary helpers, reducing copy-paste across exploration handlers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant issue is duplicate translation at the model-to-JSON seam: two independent serialization paths (handler formatXxx methods and model toMap() methods) exist for the same data, and a parallel duplication appears at the CQL substitution seam (RelationConfig vs PatternSubstitution); these indicate that integration seams were added without consolidating existing translations.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The query-to-explorer seam is well-designed and the response-building factoring is sound. However the codebase has two specific cross-layer translation duplications that force a developer to update multiple places for one schema change: the model serialization seam and the CQL lemma-substitution seam. Additionally the 'seeds' parameter naming inconsistency at the HTTP seam is a confusing API contract. These are not cosmetic issues — they affect maintainability of the boundary contracts. Score sits at 62: good seam infrastructure let down by unresolved translation duplication.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer class-level Javadoc provides a genuine algorithm walkthrough with a concrete example — adds real understanding, not noise
LogDiceCalculator comments explain the UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel distinction from 0.0, which is a non-obvious API contract
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES Javadoc lists valid input values explicitly — useful boundary documentation
CqlUtils method Javadocs include worked examples in @param/@return — appropriate for a utility with non-obvious edge cases
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The AI-generated debt is concentrated in three patterns: duplicate documentation (back-to-back Javadoc blocks), mechanical code duplication (posGroupFromConstraint parallel blocks, duplicate substitution methods), and narrative debug scaffolding (step-banner logging) — all consistent with iterative LLM-assisted development where cleanup passes were not completed.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The most definitive AI artefact is the duplicate Javadoc on buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution — two consecutive blocks with no code between them is an unambiguous editing failure. The posGroupFromConstraint parallel-block structure and the duplicate substitution implementations reinforce a pattern of mechanically replicated code rather than extracted reuse. The debug step-banners are a strong but not unique AI signal. Against this, the codebase avoids pure pass-through wrappers (except the deprecated getEdges()), has useful non-restating comments, and shows clear domain naming throughout. The score is 61: several definitive AI artefacts that would require cleanup, but not pervasive enough to dominate the whole codebase.

strengths: Deprecated overloads carry explicit @Deprecated annotation with Javadoc @deprecated tags pointing to replacement methods
The deprecated shims correctly delegate to new split methods — no functional regression
ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult model classes both renamed the public method to buildEdges() with a clear deprecated getEdges() shim, showing intentional API cleanup
The tag= backward-compat path in RelationConfig is explicitly comment-justified with language-support rationale
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The migration is largely complete and well-marked; the residual issue is a single active call site in Main.java that still invokes the deprecated overload rather than the new split methods, plus two orphaned model shims with no callers.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase shows deliberate, annotated migration work: deprecated symbols are clearly marked and the shims correctly delegate. The one concrete failure is Main.java line 219, which calls the deprecated three-argument executeDependencyPattern instead of the new zero-headPos overload, keeping the migration incomplete. The duplicate Javadoc in buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution describing a removed code path is a minor cleanup item. Overall this is a 'mostly finished migration with one forgotten call site' pattern — solid enough to score well above 60, but the active deprecated call prevents a score above 80.

strengths: Packages are small and focused: no package exceeds 9 files, and most have 2-4 files
The model package cleanly isolates all data-transfer objects, preventing DTO coupling from leaking into other layers
The config package correctly owns grammar-loading, POS group definitions, and relation type enum in one place
The indexer/blacklab sub-package correctly separates indexing from querying concerns
The exploration package keeps semantic-field algorithms away from HTTP handling
package_organization.issue_character: Two purely utility classes (PatternSubstitution, RelationUtils) live in the api package despite having no HTTP dependency, and the viz package is an undersized single-file sub-package; these are misclassification issues rather than a structural breakdown.
package_organization.score_rationale: For a 32-file Java project the package structure is well-organised: packages are small, names reflect responsibilities, and cross-cutting concerns (model, config, utils) are cleanly isolated. The main detractors are PatternSubstitution and RelationUtils living in api despite being pure utilities with zero HTTP coupling, and the viz single-file package that creates overhead without providing a meaningful boundary. These are real navigability problems (a developer looking for CQL substitution utilities would not think to look in api) but they are narrow in scope. The codebase scores above average but below 'well-organised with only cosmetic issues'.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface documents the distinction between findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern clearly — each method's scope and caller expectations are explained
LogDiceCalculator uses a named UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel (Double.NaN) and documents it explicitly so callers know to use Double.isNaN to detect the uncomputable case
GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException with specific, actionable messages for each validation failure
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() documents that it throws IllegalStateException when relationType is absent — the contract is truthful
contract_coherence.issue_character: The coherence failures cluster around documentation that describes removed or hypothetical behaviour — the %s placeholder form in both the interface Javadoc and a stale Javadoc block no longer exists in the implementation, and the single-seed endpoint documents the wrong HTTP parameter name, creating real client-facing breakage.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Several contracts in this codebase are well-stated and truthful (LogDiceCalculator, GrammarConfigLoader, QueryExecutor method distinctions). However, three specific violations directly harm developers: the QueryExecutor.findCollocations interface claims %s support that does not exist; the /api/semantic-field/explore endpoint documents 'seed' but requires 'seeds'; and executeDependencyPatternWithPos accepts null for a 'must not be null' param with silent misbehaviour. The parameter name mismatch is the most concrete — it will break real clients. These are not edge cases; they sit at primary public API boundaries. Score reflects a codebase with good intentions but measurable, high-visibility contract failures.

strengths: Guard clauses at the top of query methods (null/empty lemma checks) are used consistently and terminate early
LogDiceCalculator uses a named UNCOMPUTABLE constant rather than a magic NaN literal, making the intent of NaN checks clear
The sealed ConcordanceResult hierarchy means switch/instanceof on result types is exhaustive
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks correctly uses try-with-resources inside finally to ensure the writer is closed once even on exception paths
No dead-code paths after unconditional returns were found across the reviewed files
logic_clarity.issue_character: The main logic issue is a mathematical identity that silently makes combinedRelevanceScore and sharedCollocateScore always equal in DiscoveredNoun, producing a redundant field that adds no information — a computation that looks meaningful but is not.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is broadly clean: guard clauses are consistent, NaN handling is deliberate, and control flow is straightforward. The one concrete defect is a repeated arithmetic identity in DiscoveredNoun construction (sharedCount * avgLogDice always equals combinedRelevanceScore) which appears at two call sites, resulting in a data-model field that carries no independent information and confuses anyone who reads it. This is a narrow but real logic clarity failure. A codebase with no such issues would score in the 85-90 range; this one sits solidly above average but below exemplary.

strengths: All model DTOs (DiscoveredNoun, AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, ExploreOptions, Edge, QueryResults inner records) use concrete, correctly-typed fields with no Object or raw-type fields
Sealed ConcordanceResult interface with explicit permits provides compile-time-exhaustive polymorphism over concordance types
Optional<RelationType> used in RelationConfig prevents null-propagation for an absent relation type
Enum types (RelationType, PosGroup, RelationEdgeType) are used for all discriminated values — no raw string comparisons against known enumerations
type_safety.issue_character: The type safety gap is concentrated in the serialisation layer: 65 uses of Map<String,Object> for response building replace what could be compile-time-verified response shapes, and one LogDiceCalculator sentinel contract is silently broken in scoreHits by assigning 0.0 where NaN is the documented uncomputable signal.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's static type system handles most basic safety automatically and the model layer is well-typed. However, the entire HTTP response serialisation path is untyped: all response shapes are expressed as Map<String,Object>, which means the API contract has no compile-time verification and any key rename or type change is invisible to the type checker. Additionally, CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits silently substitutes 0.0 for LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE (NaN), breaking the contract that callers can use Double.isNaN to detect the uncomputable case. These are structural rather than incidental gaps, placing the codebase below the 70 threshold despite strong model-layer typing.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper cleanly extracts the shared collocate search/ranking pipeline from BlackLabQueryExecutor, following SRP.
ExplorationHandlers shares its common preamble logic (resolveRelationConfig, resolveExploreParams, buildBaseExploreResponse, buildExploreResponseBody) across single-seed and multi-seed handlers.
The three-phase pipeline in executeBcqlQuery (collect → score → rank) is named with clear method boundaries.
ExploreOptions and ExploreParams bundle related parameters, avoiding long argument lists.
design_coherence.issue_character: Residual structural duplication in SketchHandlers (surface vs dependency full-sketch loops) and in BlackLabQueryExecutor (findCollocations vs runDependencyQuery group-iteration), where each variation differs only in one extraction helper call.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase is well-above-average in design: it has already performed the key extractions (CollocateQueryHelper, shared explore helpers). The remaining duplication in SketchHandlers and the near-identical group-iteration loops in BlackLabQueryExecutor are real design debt — each represents an opportunity to unify via a strategy-style parameterisation. These patterns are confirmed, not false positives. Score of 71 reflects solid architectural intent with a handful of notable execution gaps.

strengths: No static initializer blocks with I/O or network side effects anywhere in the codebase.
System.getProperty for the grammar config path is read inside the command handler method, not at class-load time, making it testable.
The dependency chain (executor → helper → index) is linear and has no cycles.
Shutdown hook is registered before server construction in Main, ensuring cleanup even if the server constructor throws.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: Two hardcoded initialization-time decisions — the CORS origin and the server bind address — are not externalised as configuration, making the server unsuitable for non-localhost deployment without code changes.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the worst initialization coupling patterns (no static I/O side effects, no circular inits, no stale snapshot globals). The two real issues are the CORS origin hardcoded as a static final constant and the loopback bind address hardcoded in the constructor — both constrain deployment without providing any runtime override path. These are genuinely below the 85-point threshold but the overall picture is solid for a Java research tool of this scope.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer is comprehensively tested via a stub QueryExecutor — the exploration layer is well isolated.
Pure computation utilities (LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, CqlUtils, RelationUtils, PatternSubstitution) all have dedicated test classes.
GrammarConfigLoader and RelationConfig are tested against the actual grammar JSON file.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest correctly skips (via JUnit Assumptions) rather than failing when no index is available — avoids CI false failures.
test_strategy.issue_character: The HTTP handler success paths and the core query-execution pipeline (CollocateQueryHelper's grouped search, scoring, and BCQL concordance) have zero coverage without a live BlackLab index, leaving the most user-facing paths entirely untested in any automated context.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase has solid unit tests for its pure computation and data-model layers (~60% of files), but critical paths — HTTP handler 200 responses, collocate search and ranking without a live index, WordSketchApiServer route registration — have zero coverage. The absence of a testable abstraction over the BlackLab index means the query layer cannot be exercised without external infrastructure. This is a structurally significant gap for a production system and places the score well below the median.

strengths: The main query methods (findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos) share a consistent 4-argument signature with uniform parameter ordering.
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias provides a single canonical location for alias resolution, preventing duplicated alias maps across handlers.
ExploreOptions bundles the 5 exploration tuning parameters instead of spreading them across method arguments.
The @Deprecated annotation and migration Javadoc on the legacy executeDependencyPattern overload are clear and actionable.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The interface mixes two result shapes (WordSketchResult vs ConcordanceResult/CollocateResult) and has one method (executeBcqlQuery) with a structurally different signature from its siblings, creating a non-uniform API surface that callers must understand in detail.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The primary query methods are internally consistent, which is a genuine strength. However, executeBcqlQuery's signature asymmetry, the plural 'seeds' parameter for a single-seed endpoint, and the mixing of path vs query parameter conventions across sibling endpoints are confirmed design inconsistencies that complicate client usage. The score of 64 reflects a mostly coherent but visibly patchy surface.

strengths: Server binds to loopback only — network-level isolation provides a consistent security boundary for all endpoints.
CORS policy is applied uniformly via HttpApiUtils to every response path (JSON, error, options, binary).
Input validation patterns are applied consistently: requireParam, NumberFormatException handling, and relation-config validation are used in all handler methods that accept those parameter types.
The /health endpoint correctly requires no parameters and performs no sensitive operations.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The main gap is the absence of upper-bound validation on numeric parameters (top, limit, maxResults), which creates a consistent but missing control — every endpoint is equally vulnerable to unbounded resource requests.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This codebase has no auth system because its domain (single-user research tool, loopback only) does not require one — penalising the absence of auth would be a category error. Within its actual trust boundary, validation is applied consistently and the CORS policy is uniformly enforced. The score of 77 is anchored by the absence of numeric upper-bound guards, which is the one real consistency gap: all endpoints that accept 'top', 'limit', or 'maxResults' could benefit from a shared cap (e.g., max 500), but none implement it.

issues.summary: Model serialization logic duplicated in ExplorationHandlers, creating two competing API representations
issues.suggestion: Choose one serialization path: either delegate entirely to model.toMap() in API handlers, or remove toMap() from the model classes and keep serialization in the API layer. The model toMap() approach is preferable since it co-locates JSON shape with data ownership.
evidence: ExplorationResult.toMap() serializes seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates, and edges.
ExplorationHandlers has private methods formatSeedCollocate(), formatDiscoveredNoun(), formatCoreCollocate(), formatEdge() that construct identical map shapes independently.
buildExploreResponseBody() in ExplorationHandlers manually iterates result.getSeedCollocates() rather than delegating to result.toMap().
If field names in the JSON response need to change, both the model toMap() and the handler format* methods must be updated separately.

issues.summary: com.sun.net.httpserver used without abstraction, coupling server to JDK-internal API
issues.suggestion: Add proper imports for com.sun.net.httpserver.* to reduce noise. For boundary integrity, introduce a thin RouteHandler functional interface in the api package that can be adapted to any underlying server, isolating the transport choice behind the api package boundary.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer stores com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer as a field without an import statement — type is referenced 9 times using its full package path inline.
registerGetHandler, registerPostHandler, and registerHandler all accept com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler without abstraction.
Any migration to a different HTTP server (Jetty, Undertow, etc.) requires rewriting WordSketchApiServer and the handler interfaces.

issues.summary: CLAUDE.md documents SemanticFieldExplorer as being in query/ but it is in exploration/

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency throws unchecked IllegalStateException in an IOException-declared method
issues.suggestion: Change the catch in getTotalFrequency to wrap as IOException: 'throw new IOException("Unexpected state retrieving frequency for lemma '" + lemma + "'", e)' — consistent with all other error-wrapping patterns in the query layer.
evidence: getTotalFrequency is declared 'throws IOException' in QueryExecutor interface.
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency catches RuntimeException and rethrows as IllegalStateException (unchecked), not IOException.

runDependencyQuery lines 148-163: iterates groups, calls BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateLemma, merges into freqMap/lemmaPosMap, then calls buildAndRankCollocates.
Both methods share the same merge pattern (freqMap.merge(key, group.size(), Long::sum)) and the same conditional pos extraction.

issues.summary: buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution has two identical Javadoc comment blocks consecutive
issues.suggestion: Remove the first (now-stale) Javadoc block; retain only the second, updated version.
evidence: Two consecutive /** ... */ Javadoc blocks appear immediately before the private static buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution method; both describe the same canonical vs legacy format semantics.

issues.summary: CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN is a hardcoded static final constant with no runtime override path
issues.suggestion: Replace the static constant with a configurable value: read System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin', 'http://localhost:3000') once at class-load time or pass it through WordSketchApiServer's constructor and inject it into HttpApiUtils via a static setter or a dedicated CorsConfig parameter.
evidence: HttpApiUtils line: static final String CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = "http://localhost:3000";
Used directly in sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse — all response paths.
There is no system property, environment variable, or constructor injection to change this value at startup.

issues.summary: Server always binds to loopback; bind address is not configurable at startup
issues.suggestion: Add an optional --host parameter in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand (defaulting to 127.0.0.1) and pass the InetAddress to WordSketchApiServer's constructor so it can be overridden for non-localhost deployments.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor: new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand parses --port but has no --host or --bind option.

issues.summary: HTTP handler 200-response paths are entirely untested; only 400 validation errors are exercised
issues.suggestion: Add handler-level unit tests that inject a stub QueryExecutor (like the one in SemanticFieldExplorerTest) into SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers via the existing constructors, use MockExchange, and assert status 200 plus key JSON field presence.
evidence: HandlersTest contains only 400-response assertions (missing parameter, etc.).
No test passes a stub/mock QueryExecutor to a handler and verifies that a 200 response is returned with well-formed JSON.
handleFullSketch, handleRelationSketch, handleSemanticFieldExplore, and handleSemanticFieldComparison all have untested success paths.

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer has no test class — route registration, method enforcement, and CORS handling are uncovered
issues.suggestion: Add WordSketchApiServerTest: start the server on a random port with a stub executor, issue HTTP requests (GET, POST, OPTIONS) and verify status codes and CORS headers using java.net.http.HttpClient.
evidence: No test file named WordSketchApiServerTest (or similar) exists in src/test/.
registerHandler logic (OPTIONS handling, 405 on wrong method) is exercised only at runtime.
wrapHandler error-catching logic is untested — IOException and RuntimeException branches are never exercised.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch, buildAndRankCollocates, and executeBcqlQuery have no unit tests without a live index
issues.suggestion: Extract buildAndRankCollocates to accept a LongUnaryOperator for frequency lookup so it can be unit-tested without a BlackLabIndex. Add tests for the NaN guard, minLogDice filtering, and result ordering.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelperTest only tests the static extractCollocateLemma method.

buildAndRankCollocates contains branching logic (NaN check, minLogDice filter, sorting, limiting) that could be tested without a real index by injecting a mock that returns a known getTotalFrequency value.
scoreHits (phase 2 of executeBcqlQuery) has conditional logDice computation and over-fetch logic that are untested.

issues.summary: executeBcqlQuery lacks a minLogDice parameter unlike all sibling query methods
issues.suggestion: Add a minLogDice parameter to executeBcqlQuery (defaulting to 0.0 via an overload or default method), and pass it through to scoreHits in CollocateQueryHelper to filter before returning.
evidence: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern all accept (lemma, ..., double minLogDice, int maxResults).
executeBcqlQuery(String bcqlPattern, int maxResults) has no minLogDice — callers cannot request score-filtered BCQL results; they must post-filter.
CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits computes logDice per hit but there is no minLogDice guard in the pipeline.

issues.summary: Single-seed explore endpoint requires a query parameter named 'seeds' (plural) but accepts only one value
issues.suggestion: Rename the parameter to 'seed' (singular) in handleSemanticFieldExplore to match the documented single-seed semantics; update the Javadoc comment accordingly. Keep 'seeds' only for the multi-seed endpoint.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore calls HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "seeds") and assigns the result to String seed.
The API documentation comment says: ?seed=house&relation=adj_predicate but the code requires 'seeds' (plural).
Clients using the documented 'seed' parameter name will receive a 400 error.

issues.summary: The deprecated 5-argument executeDependencyPattern overload remains in the public interface, requiring implementation by all stubs
issues.suggestion: Remove the 5-arg deprecated overload from the QueryExecutor interface; add a minor-version note to CLAUDE.md or a CHANGELOG entry explaining the removal. If backward compatibility with external callers is needed, retain only as a static utility method outside the interface.
evidence: QueryExecutor declares a @Deprecated default method executeDependencyPattern(lemma, deprel, headPosConstraint, minLogDice, maxResults) that delegates to the non-deprecated variants.

SemanticFieldExplorerTest.StubExecutor implements executeDependencyPattern (2-arg) and executeDependencyPatternWithPos but also inherits the deprecated default — test stubs are coupled to a method that has no callers.
No production code calls the 5-arg form per codebase search.

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams URL-decodes values but not keys, silently dropping percent-encoded parameter names
issues.suggestion: No action needed — this was a false positive upon closer inspection. Both keys and values are URL-decoded correctly.
evidence: parseQueryParams splits on '&' and '=', then calls URLDecoder.decode on keyValue[1] (value) and keyValue[0] (key).
Wait — re-reading the code: params.put(URLDecoder.decode(keyValue[0], 'UTF-8'), URLDecoder.decode(keyValue[1], 'UTF-8')). Both key and value are decoded. This is correct.
False positive: both key and value are decoded in the same try block.

issues.summary: Numeric parameters (top, limit, maxResults) have no upper-bound validation across any endpoint, allowing unbounded resource requests
issues.suggestion: Add a shared MAX_TOP / MAX_LIMIT constant (e.g., 500) in HttpApiUtils or a new RequestParamValidator class, and apply it consistently in all parameter-parsing calls: if (top > MAX_TOP) { sendError(exchange, 400, 'top exceeds maximum of ' + MAX_TOP); return null; }
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams: top = Integer.parseInt(params.getOrDefault('top', '15')); no upper bound check.
SketchHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples: limit = Integer.parseInt(params.getOrDefault('limit', '10')); no upper bound check.
A request with ?top=1000000 would be accepted and forwarded to SemanticFieldExplorer, potentially running 1,000,000 collocate queries against the index.
